---
title: "Energetics of Combustion Reactions Reference Page<br/>*Fueling the Fire: The Shocking Truth About Combustion Reactions!*"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
{{< include /LinkKey.qmd >}}
# Intro to *Fueling the Fire: The Shocking Truth About Combustion Reactions!*

Combustion reactions are among the most important energy-producing processes in engineering and everyday life.  
They power engines, generate electricity, heat buildings, and drive many industrial processes.

To analyze combustion systems, we apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** together with tabulated thermodynamic data such as heats of formation and heats of combustion. This page introduces the basic terminology used to describe fuels, heating values, and combustion behavior.

## Fueling the Fire: The Shocking Truth About Combustion Reactions!

This video introduces common types of fuels and explains the thermodynamic quantities used to characterize combustion, including heating values and combustion energetics.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/lW5dK-F6H3U?si=hL4Y6LfStMqKtkLR" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](110-FuelsHeatingVisuals.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

[Fuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuel)  
: A substance that reacts with oxygen in an exothermic reaction, releasing energy in the form of heat.

[Solid Fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_fuel)  
: Fuels in the solid state, such as coal, coke ([pyrolyzed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pyrolysis) coal), wood, or charcoal ([pyrolyzed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pyrolysis) wood).

[Liquid Fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_fuel)  
: Fuels in the liquid state such as kerosene, methanol, or gasoline.

[Gaseous Fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuel_gas)  
: Fuels in the gaseous state such as natural gas or acetylene.

[Coal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coal)  
: A naturally occurring solid fuel formed from vegetable matter subjected to heat and pressure over geologic time. Major classifications include anthracite, bituminous, subbituminous, and lignite.

[Enthalpy or Heat of Combustion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_of_combustion)  
: The enthalpy change associated with the complete combustion of a fixed amount of fuel. It may be expressed on a per-mass or per-mole basis.

[Lower Heating Value (LHV)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_of_combustion#:~:text=Higher%20heating%20value-,Lower%20heating%20value,-Gross%20heating%20value)  
: The heating value obtained when water formed during combustion remains in the **vapor phase**. The heating value is the negative of the heat of combustion under this assumption.

[Higher Heating Value (HHV)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_of_combustion#Higher_heating_value)  
: The heating value obtained when water formed during combustion is assumed to **condense to liquid water**.  

Because condensation releases additional heat, the HHV is larger than the LHV.

[Adiabatic Flame Temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adiabatic_flame_temperature)  
: The maximum temperature reached by a fuel–air mixture if combustion occurs with **no heat transfer to the surroundings**.

[Flash Point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flash_point)  
: The temperature at which the vapor above a liquid fuel forms a combustible mixture with air that will ignite if exposed to an ignition source.

[Autoignition Temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autoignition_temperature)  
: The temperature at which a fuel–air mixture will spontaneously ignite without an external ignition source.

[Flammability Limits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flammability_limit)  
: The range of fuel–air compositions that can support combustion.  

Below the **lower flammability limit (LFL)** there is too little fuel to sustain combustion.  
Above the **upper flammability limit (UFL)** there is too little oxygen to sustain combustion.

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos

### Two videos back

**Germain Hess and the Enthalpy of Doom**<br/>
*Hess's Law and $\Delta \hat{H}^\circ_f$, $\Delta \hat{H}^\circ_r$, and $\Delta \hat{H}_c$*<br/>
[Video](https://youtu.be/qdgH4v64vOQ), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/HeatsOfFormationAndReactionVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/104-HeatsOfReactionVisuals.qmd)

### Previous video

**Chemical Reactions vs. The First Law: Who Wins?**<br/>
*Forms of the 1^st^ Law for Chemical Reactions*<br/>
[Video](https://youtu.be/mFF5hDHclPE), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/The1stLawReactingSystems.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/106-1stLawReactingVisuals.qmd)

### Next video

**React Hard: The First Law Gets Chemical**<br/>
*Example of the 1^st^ Law for Chemical Reactions*<br/>
[Video](https://youtu.be/q-W5Y1d85go), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/Example1stLawForReactingSystemsVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/111-EnergeticsOfReactionsPt1Visuals.qmd)

### Two videos forward

**React Harder: Formation Heats and Alternate Endings**<br/>
*2 more Examples of the 1^st^ Law for Chemical Reactions*<br/>
[Video](https://youtu.be/iqxJP6g-Qsc), [Info Page](../../balances/energy/Example1stLawForReactingSystemsVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/energy/112-EnergeticsOfReactionsPt2Visuals.qmd)
